text: The surgeon applies tension to the omentum over the second part of the duodenum, preparing the area for further dissection.
The surgeon uses the harmonic scalpel to place a gauze piece inside the surgical field.
The surgeon exposes the second part of the duodenum, making it clearly visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon elevates the mesentery while using gauze for dissection, positioning the duodenum downward to create a retroperitoneal space.
The surgeon uses gauze and carbon dioxide insufflation to create a clear retroperitoneal space, revealing anatomical structures in the surgical field.

The surgeon applies traction to the mesentery while pushing the duodenum downwards, creating space in the retroperitoneal area. A small incision is made just below the right colligatory, using gauze for dissection.

The surgeon exposes the third part of the duodenum, removing fat from above the vessel to reveal the right colligatory artery, which arises from the superior mesenteric artery. Clips are placed proximally and distally on the artery.
The surgeon continues dissection towards the lateral abdominal wall, revealing the gonadal vessels. Careful dissection exposes the ureter, which is positioned medial to the gonadal vessels.
The surgeon positions the ureter and gonadal vessels anteriorly, ensuring they remain visible and protected during the dissection.
The surgeon visually confirms the dissection of the right colligatory vessel, ensuring it is adequately exposed and separated from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon pushes the colon upwards, gradually exposing the lateral abdominal wall.
The surgeon observes the ureter turning downwards and the gonadal vessels descending, while continuing dissection towards the lateral abdominal wall.
The surgeon completes the medial to lateral dissection by dividing the vessel, then begins the lateral to medial dissection, applying medial traction to the appendix and cecum.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to make an incision in the lateral abdominal wall fold, while carbon dioxide insufflation aids in maintaining a clear surgical field. Continuous traction is applied to the cecum, facilitating the dissection and keeping the area bloodless.
The surgeon continues the dissection towards the hepatic flexure of the colon, with the colon visibly pushed medially and downwards.
The surgeon exposes the liver and gallbladder, visible in the surgical field after completing the dissection of the colon.
The surgeon begins the dissection of the transverse colon, focusing on separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon separates the transverse colon from the mesocolon, visibly detaching the colon from its mesenteric attachments.
The surgeon applies upward traction on the mesocolon while pushing the transverse colon downwards, facilitating the separation of the transverse colon from the mesocolon.
The transverse colon is completely divided, with the surrounding tissue visibly separated and the colon pushed medially and downwards.
The surgeon separates the remnant of the transverse mesocolon from the colon and proceeds to reach for the medial colic vessel.
The surgeon clips and divides the right branch of the middle colic vein using a harmonic device, followed by the division of the right branch of the middle colic artery.
The surgeon clips the separated section of the intestine with two clips proximally and one distally, then divides it between the clips, preparing the site for intestinal anastomosis.
The surgeon makes a small incision to remove the specimen, performing the anastomosis extracorporeally between the transverse colon and the ileum.
